title: Electrical Spin Pumping in Exchange-coupled Molecules
authors: Paul Greule, Wantong Huang, Kwan Ho Au-Yeung, Máté Stark, Johannes Schwenk, Christoph Sürgers, Wolfgang Wernsdorfer, Philip Willke
abstract: Electron spins in single molecules are a promising platform for quantum information processing. However, their practical implementation as qubits requires reliable control at the single-entity level, including an efficient state initialization. Here, we demonstrate the remote, all-electrical initialization of the electron spin in single molecules: Using electron spin resonance scanning tunneling microscopy, we investigate coupled pairs of S=1/2 molecules (Fe-FePc), where one molecule serves as a readout and pumping unit for the neighboring one. We show that the exchange interaction between them enables angular momentum transfer, which allows for the control of the remote spin state via the direction and magnitude of the spin-polarized tunneling current and the exchange coupling strength. These results establish a general, all-electrical approach for remote spin initialization that is readily transferable to a wide range of spin-based quantum architectures.
summary: The researchers demonstrated a way to remotely initialize the spin state of a molecule using an electrical current from an STM tip. By using exchange coupling between two molecules, they showed that angular momentum can be transferred to a 'remote' spin, allowing for all-electrical control. This approach provides a scalable method for initializing qubits in molecular-based quantum architectures.
main_problem: The need for reliable, efficient, and all-electrical initialization of electron spin states in single molecules for quantum information processing, overcoming thermal equilibrium limits.
main_result: Demonstrated remote, all-electrical initialization of a single electron spin using spin-polarized tunneling current, where exchange coupling enables angular momentum transfer to a remote spin.
method: Electron Spin Resonance Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (ESR-STM) was used to probe spin transitions, combined with rate equation modeling and second-order tunneling simulations.
model_or_system: A pair of S=1/2 molecules (Fe-FePc) on an MgO/Ag(100) surface, modeled by an effective spin Hamiltonian including Zeeman terms and anisotropic exchange coupling (J_parallel and J_perp).
key_observables: Resonance frequencies, peak amplitudes, remote spin magnetization (M_B), and change in tunneling current (delta I).
important_parameters: Exchange coupling strength (|J_perp| approx 220-621 MHz), tip spin polarization (eta approx 0.2-0.5), and spin lifetime (T1 approx 1-10 microseconds).
main_assumptions: The sensing spin is assumed to fully adapt to the tip's spin polarization; dipole-dipole interactions are neglected and absorbed into effective exchange parameters.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows voltage dependence of ESR spectra; Figure B shows ESR spectra at avoided level crossing; Figure C shows remote spin magnetization vs. tunneling current and the relationship between I0 and exchange coupling; Figure S8/S9 show temperature-dependent pumping and spin chain transfer simulations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of spin initialization, describes the ESR-STM experimental setup and molecular system, presents the experimental demonstration of remote spin pumping, provides a theoretical model for the spin dynamics and exchange-mediated transfer, and concludes with discussions on scalability and temperature effects.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a practical mechanism for non-equilibrium state preparation in a coupled spin system, which is highly relevant to open quantum systems and the control of many-body spin dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Measurement scheme. (A) Schematic illustration of the tunnel junction with two molecule spins assembled on MgO/Ag(100). For ESR measurements an external magnetic field B is applied and a magnetic tip apex is used. A DC bias voltage VDC and a radio-frequency voltage VRF are applied to the tip which is positioned above the sensing spin SA to probe and drive its spin state. SA is exchange coupled to a second, remote spin SB. (B) STM topography of a molecule pair [Image conditions: 3 nm x 3 nm, I = 10 pA, VDC = −100 mV]. The cross marks the measurement position used in (D). (C) Energy level diagram of the spin system, showing the configurations with the remote spin SB in the ground...

Figure 2: Voltage dependence of the ESR spectra. Change in tunneling current ∆I as a function of radio-frequency shift δf = f −f0 for different VDC at constant conductance setpoint Gset [ESR conditions: same as in Fig. 1D]. The traces are normalized and vertically offset for clarity. In addition, they are centered at their resonance frequencies to compensate for the voltage induced frequency shift arising from spin-electric coupling[26]. Colored dots show the experimental data and black solid lines represent fits to a double Fano function. The insets illustrate the tunnel junction with the direction of the tunneling current for positive and negative VDC.

Figure 3: Remote spin pumping in a molecule pair. (A) Spin excitation: Schematic of the tunnel junction illustrating the remote spin excitation towards |↓↓⟩of the exchange coupled spins driven by the spin-polarized tunneling current for I < 0. (B) Energy level diagram of the coupled spin system. Flip-flop (FF) and remote scattering (RS) transitions that dominate for VDC < 0 are indicated by red arrows. ESR detection is shown in orange. (C) Extracted occupations of the ground |g⟩and excited state |e⟩of SB as a function of VDC and I at constant Gset. Data points are obtained from ESR frequency sweeps as presented in Fig. 1D [ESR conditions: Gset = 0.5 nS, B = 450 mT, VRF = 12 mV]. (D)...

Figure 4: Spin pumping for different exchange interaction strengths. (A) STM to- pographies of four molecule pairs (I −IV). Colored crosses mark the measurement positions used in B and C [Image conditions: 3 nm x 3 nm, I = 10-20 pA, VDC = ±100 mV]. (B) Corresponding ESR spectra recorded at the avoided level crossing (δ = 0) showing ∆I as a function of radio frequency shift δf. Grey dots represent experimental data, solid lines are fits using a four peak Fano function. For clarity, the spectra are vertically offset.
